query: What is the difference between visibility hidden and display none?

competitors.label: visibility
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical MDN reference for visibility and is the strongest official baseline for comparing hidden-but-laid-out elements against display-driven removal.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on visibility semantics, layout participation, and accessibility-tree effects.
theirGaps: The property reference is less interview-shaped and less explicit about the practical choose-between-two-tools decision developers make when hiding UI.

realWorldUseCases: Both visibility and display control element rendering in CSS, but they behave differently. visibility: hidden hides the element visually while preserving its layout space, whereas display: none removes the element completely from the document flow.
followUpConfusion:
Overview

The visibility and display properties determine whether elements appear and how they affect layout. They differ in whether the element continues to occupy space or not.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains visibility accurately, but it is lighter on the concrete UI decision of whether you want hidden space preserved or layout removed entirely.
actionableExamples: It includes solid property examples and notes on visibility behavior, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the follow-up confusion teams hit first: layout space, accessibility-tree consequences, and animation or toggle trade-offs.

actionableExamples: Keep one side-by-side comparison example visible so the answer stays faster to scan than a property reference.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical visibility reference, but our page is stronger as an interview answer because it turns visibility-versus-display into a concrete layout-space and hiding-strategy decision.
